comments:
Summary:
This paper addresses online (sequential) nonparametric regression by proposing sequential versions of kernel and local polynomial estimators where each incoming datum is incorporated with a shrinking bandwidth ht (ht = c t^{-1/(2d+1)}). The main claims are (i) these sequential estimators can be updated in linear time in the number of grid points and attain the same minimax rates as their batch counterparts, (ii) an exponential-weighting (expert mixing) scheme lets the method adapt to unknown smoothness, and (iii) a sequential backfitting extension is outlined for additive models. The paper contains asymptotic risk analyses for density estimation and regression (sketches/proofs in appendices), and simulation studies comparing sequential and batch estimators.

Strengths:
- Clear, well-motivated problem: online updating for nonparametric regression with reduced computational cost.
- Natural and simple algorithmic idea: assign each observation the bandwidth appropriate to its arrival time and update sufficient quantities via rank-one updates (Woodbury), giving O(|G| d^2) per observation for a grid of prediction points.
- Theoretical contribution: asymptotic risk analysis showing that the sequential estimator achieves the standard minimax rates n^{-2d/(2d+1)}. The extension to adaptation via exponential weighting and a statement of oracle inequality is useful.
- Practical extensions (mixing/adaptation, additive models) are discussed and an empirical comparison to batch estimators is provided.

Weaknesses / Concerns / Suggestions:
- The iid assumption is strong. Many online settings have nonstationarity or dependent observations; discussion or experiments on robustness would strengthen the work.
- Finite-sample behavior and constants: the theoretical analysis is asymptotic; more attention to finite-sample constants, numerical stability (e.g., conditioning of X^T W X when early bandwidths are large), and choice of the constant c in ht would be helpful. The experiments use a small sample size (n=150); larger-scale experiments and runtime comparisons would be informative.
- The backfitting (additive model) algorithm is presented but no convergence guarantees are provided; numerical behavior and complexity per update should be explored in more depth.
- Some technical conditions could be made clearer (for instance the restriction lim_{n->∞} 1/n^2 ∑_{t=1}^n 1/ht = 0 — clarify its role and plausibility for parameter choices). Also clarify memory costs when many grid points |G| are used, and how to choose the grid in practice.
- The experiments are limited: mostly low-dimensional, moderate n, and the comparison set could include other single-pass online nonparametric approaches (e.g., RKHS online algorithms) or incremental smoothing schemes for a stronger empirical case.
- Presentation issues: there are duplicated/garbled paragraphs toward the end of the provided text and minor typos. Proofs are sketched in places; ensure appendices fully contain the arguments.

Overall assessment & recommendation:
This is a solid, well-motivated contribution that combines a simple algorithmic trick with asymptotic analysis to obtain computationally efficient online nonparametric regression with minimax guarantees. It is appropriate for the audience and likely to be of interest to researchers working on streaming/online estimation and nonparametric smoothing. Before publication, I recommend tightening the presentation, expanding empirical evaluation (larger n, sensitivity to c, runtime/stability), and clarifying technical conditions and practical implementation details (conditioning, grid choice, memory). With those improvements the paper would make a valuable contribution.
